title: Proverbs
author: Solomon (chs. 1–29), Agur (ch. 30), Lemuel's mother (ch. 31); collected under Solomon's patronage
date: ~950 BC (Solomon's era); final compilation possibly ~700 BC (Hezekiah's men, 25:1)
setting: A royal wisdom school, addressing a young man preparing for life. The book presents Wisdom as a divine gift available to all who fear the LORD.
purpose: To impart practical, God-centered wisdom for navigating all of life — family, work, speech, wealth, friendship — on the foundation that 'the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom' (1:7; 9:10).
themes: The fear of the LORD as wisdom's foundation
Speech: words that heal vs. words that destroy
Sexual purity and faithfulness in marriage
The Excellent Wife (ch. 31) as Wisdom embodied

outline.label: In Praise of Wisdom: Discourses to a Son
Agur, Lemuel, and the Excellent Wife

before.label: Creation: God establishes the order that wisdom discerns
Psalms — Israel's hymnbook and wisdom poetry

after.label: Ecclesiastes — wisdom tested to its limits
Jesus as Wisdom incarnate — 'something greater than Solomon'
James — the NT wisdom letter applying Proverbs' ethics

key_people.name: Solomon
key_people.role: Primary author and compiler; his God-given wisdom (1 Kings 3) establishes the foundational premise that wisdom is a gift from YHWH, not merely shrewd human insight

key_people.name: Agur son of Jakeh
key_people.role: Contributor of chapter 30, whose self-deprecating opening ('I am too stupid to be a man') models the epistemological humility that is the beginning of wisdom

key_people.name: King Lemuel's Mother
key_people.role: The unnamed woman whose oracle closes the book (ch. 31) and whose teaching culminates in the portrait of the worthy woman — wisdom's embodiment in daily life

context: Proverbs was compiled in its final form during the reign of Hezekiah (25:1), though its core material derives from the Solomonic era (10th century BC). It belongs to the international wisdom tradition of the ancient Near East but is thoroughly Yahwistic: wisdom is not abstract philosophy but the skill of living in alignment with the created order that YHWH has established. The book addresses a young man entering adult life and aims to form his character across every domain — speech, work, sexuality, wealth, and friendship.
christ_connection: Proverbs 8's personification of Wisdom — present at creation, delighting before God, calling all people to life — is the primary OT background for John's Logos prologue (John 1:1–14) and Paul's description of Christ as 'the wisdom of God' (1 Cor 1:24, 30; Col 2:3). Jesus embodies not only wisdom's teaching but wisdom's person: he is the one in whom all the treasures of wisdom are hidden (Col 2:3), and the Sermon on the Mount is his extended wisdom discourse on true flourishing in the kingdom.

key_verses.note: 'The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom' — the programmatic statement that frames all that follows: wisdom is relational before it is intellectual
'Trust in the LORD with all your heart... and he will make straight your paths' — the summary of what it means to live wisely: active trust that displaces self-sufficiency
Lady Wisdom's self-description as God's co-worker in creation — the deepest theological claim of the book and the OT's richest anticipation of Christ as Wisdom incarnate
'A woman who fears the LORD is to be praised' — the conclusion that ties the whole book together: the worthy woman embodies the same fear of the LORD that opened in 1:7

themes_detail.title: Fear of the LORD as Wisdom's Foundation
themes_detail.text: The phrase 'fear of the LORD' occurs fourteen times in Proverbs and is not an emotion to be overcome but a posture to be cultivated. It combines reverential awe, moral seriousness, and relational trust — the recognition that God is the creator and sustainer of the order in which we live. Without this orientation, human cleverness becomes mere cunning; with it, even ordinary decisions about speech and work and money are made in the light of reality. The fear of the LORD is thus not the opposite of wisdom but its irreplaceable precondition.

themes_detail.title: Wisdom and Folly as Competing Invitations
themes_detail.text: Lady Wisdom (chs. 1–9) and the Strange/Foolish Woman are not merely allegorical contrasts but competing life orientations that issue invitations to the same young man. Both call from public places (Wisdom from the gates; Folly from her door); both promise pleasure and satisfaction. The difference is in the destination: Wisdom's house is life; Folly's leads to Sheol. This structural contrast frames every subsequent proverb as a choice between two possible orientations toward God, creation, and one's own nature.

themes_detail.title: Speech as the Index of Character
themes_detail.text: More proverbs address speech than any other single topic — lying, flattery, gossip, harsh words, gentle answers, timely words, and rash vows. This is not arbitrary but reflects the theological conviction that speech is the primary domain in which the heart's condition is revealed (cf. Matt 12:34). Wise speech builds up, restrains, tells the truth, and considers its timing; foolish speech tears down, flatters for advantage, and speaks without restraint. The discipline of the tongue is both the fruit of wisdom and an arena in which wisdom is practised and formed.

themes_detail.title: Work, Wealth, and the Dignity of the Ordinary
themes_detail.text: Proverbs refuses the Greek disdain for manual labor and commercial life: the diligent farmer, the skilful craftsman, and the wise merchant are celebrated. Wealth is not evil but must be held with open hands — it comes from God, can be lost, and must never become the foundation of identity or security. The poor are to be treated with dignity (17:5; 22:2), and oppressing them is an affront to their Maker. Proverbs envisions an economic life structured by honesty, diligence, generosity, and justice — a vision that anticipates the shalom of the kingdom.
